verdict_reasoning: VEGFA is a secreted homodimeric glycoprotein with no transmembrane domain. Its canonical signal peptide drives it into the secretory pathway and it is released extracellularly as a soluble growth factor (or deposited into the ECM via heparin-binding). Multiple major isoforms (VEGF121, VEGF165, VEGF189, VEGF206) differ in heparin-binding affinity and ECM retention but none encode a stable transmembrane anchor. Checking contextual buckets: (1) cell_state_induced — VEGFA is upregulated by hypoxia/tumors but remains secreted, not surface-displayed; (2) tissue_restricted_surface — no evidence of surface display in any lineage; (3) lysosomal_exocytosis — not applicable; (4) dual_localization — no documented PM-resident pool by surface biotinylation or flow on intact cells; (5) stable_surface_attachment — heparan-sulfate proteoglycan binding on cell surfaces is reversible/wash-sensitive and constitutes matrix deposition, not wash-resistant PM anchoring. Therapeutic programs (bevacizumab, ranibizumab, aflibercept) all target the circulating/soluble pool, not cell-surface VEGFA. No isoform acts as a type-I TM precursor. Verdict: secreted_only.
